replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Very simple practice, created by psychologists, but very effective: https://focusing.org/sixsteps

Focusing teaches you to process emotions by assigning words, phrases, or symbols of any kind to physical discomforts in the body--which causes a form of relaxation to occur.

It's also superb training for mediumship. I went from focusing on the body to focusing on the bodies' energy centers (you can use chakras or the sephiroth), to focusing on the spirits I invoke for ritual practice, and now I can use it with just about anything.

You need to create positive energy in yourself before you have any to give to the world.

comments.title: Thanking the gods

Happy new year!

I have recently experienced something amazing and life-transforming which I had thought impossible, so I had hardly dared pray for it - but it goes a long way to solving or improving most other problems which I did. I want to thank all the gods I have prayed to or invoked over the last 18 months, this is quite a lot.

It is also Imbolc tomorrow and though I mostly follow Greek gods, I do feel an affinity with the wheel of the year, and this seems like a good time to start on more of the thanking. Last week for example I offered some beer to Odin (one of the non-Greek gods I have prayed to and invoked) at the hour and day of Mercury. I opened the beer and did the speaking part indoors and then poured it in a casual way that hopefully looked, if anyone noticed, like I was just shaking out a bottle to empty it before putting it in the recycling bin.

I plan to do more thanking later when I can get to places such as sacred sites, but feel I must make a start as that could be months away.

replies.content:
You said:
“Do you have advice for how to stay sane when it seems like basically everyone around you has lost your mind?... I'm stuck here with no income and no chance of getting my own place until [May]”

1) This could be a time when you learn a new form of exercise. Tai chi perhaps. Or working out with small weights. You can find instructions via YouTube. (You can fill a plastic drink bottle with water to make an informal weight.)

2) Is there a natural landscape nearby? Long walks.

3) In addition to prayer, you might try some form of working with the mind, observing the mind making friends with the mind. One basic exercise that’s found in many traditions is to put your attention on your breath. Notice, breath comes in breath goes out breath comes in breath goes out – whoops now I’m thinking about something irritating! Return mind to the breath. Notice how mind tends to go in many directions – that’s how minds are.

Some forms of this practice invite you to pay attention to how your breath feels. Is it deep? Is it shallow? Not to change anything, but just to be in touch with this part of our physical being.

I’m sure there are many forms of these practices in JMG’s books but I don’t know them well enough to mention specific examples.

4) Another form of this practice is to gaze at a beautiful or inspiring object. I bet you can find some forms of nature to bring back to your room. A flower, a tree branch, a rock with strange markings. Rest your gaze on the object. Then relax. Then gaze again.

In general when you’re starting to do mind training, do short sessions. Then break for a cup of tea or 10 minutes of stretching. Then another short session of sitting practice.

5) Something that might be helpful for this time in your life or really for any period of difficulty, is to pray for those who are experiencing similar difficulties. Right now so many people find they can’t talk with relatives or former close friends because they now live in a different reality. People are stuck in place due to virus lockdowns, etc. It’s possible to pray for yourself and also those who have similar, and worse, situations. May our minds be blessed with wisdom, compassion, peace. May circumstances change. May these problems dissolve like clouds.

6) There’s a way of practicing for yourself and then gradually expanding your practice outwards.
You could visualize wisdom and goodness as a being of white light. Or use any other image that works for you. You’re in your ordinary form. You visualize rays of light blessing you, flowing through you, washing away any neurosis, the effects of any past negative actions, any illness. You visualize light filling you up.

Then you start to send out light to other people, other beings. Rays of light could go out from your heart to one special person that you love. Or to one person you know who is dealing with illness. Or to many people – to all the people who live in this house. To all the people who live in this city.

But don’t push it too far. Be aware. It’s like anything else, you start small and stay aware of what you can do sincerely – don’t strain to push it further.

7) Whatever practice you do, if possible begin with the intention to benefit other beings. Whatever practice you do, do it with a light touch. Don’t make a big deal of it. At the end of any practice, think “this practice I have done, may it benefit all beings.”

from EllenZ

Remember that we are made in the image of the gods, and our genders thus reflect theirs, though of course imperfectly and incompletely. What do you need to know? replies.content: Thanks, I was wondering if the masculine represented “Spirit” in Western magic, and the feminine represented “Maya”? This is perhaps simplistic and inaccurate? This is how I view the Eastern Divine genders sometimes...

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content: Western magic doesn't use the standard Hindu division of spirit vs. maya; it's more a matter of essence vs. substance.  If you work with Hindu deities, though, you're better off using Hindu definitions!

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content:
Perfect. Thank you! Actually, I do work with a Western system that uses the term Essence, although juxtaposed with “Personality”.
Last question: what is the definition of “substance”, please?

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content: Essence and substance are the two expressions of every phenomenon in classical Western philosophy. Essence is the abstract nature of a thing; substance is the actual form it takes in practice. The essence of a cup, say, is the set of abstract relationships that make it a cup; the substance of a cup is the actual cup sitting on your table. ("Substance" is from Latin "substantia," literally "that which stands under" an abstract essence.)

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content: I love that! Much appreciated!!!

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content: Welcome back!  If the essence of the cup is its nwyfre and the substance is its gwyar, is there any classic Western occult term for its calas?

replies.title: Re: The God and Goddess in Magic
replies.content: Thank you. Nah, the essence is nwyfre but the substance is calas; what resolves the binary is the nature of the thing, which is gwyar. Essentia, substantia, and natura make up one of the classic ternaries in old-fashioned occult philosophy.

comments.title: Deities from different pantheons
comments.content: Since embracing a polytheistic view while remaining a practicing Catholic, I've felt a growing call from Deities that belong to different cultural pantheons.  Primarily, Gods and Goddesses from Roman, Celtic, and Canaanite pantheons.  After wrestling with what to do, I've recently decided to offer dulia to these different Deities and try to form relationships with Them.  I am wondering if I should maintain a separate, smaller shrine for each culture or if using a single, larger shrine would be okay?  For example, would a single shrine with statues of Christ, Minerva, Danu, and Anat be a good idea or do such different Deities and cultures generally prefer separate shrines?  And would rituals that offer something to each Deity, say an act of latria to Christ and acts of dulia to other Deities, be an acceptable ritual?  I've encountered conflicting information (especially when adding my Patron God, Jesus Christ) into the mix.

replies.title: Re: Deities from different pantheons
replies.content: You can put them in one big shrine, but if you have an image of Christ, it should go above the others to symbolize the difference in your approach. Rituals of the sort you've described have been quite common in some contexts -- I'm thinking here among others of Alexander Severus, one of the good Roman emperors, who had an image of Christ on his personal altar along with other deities, and offered prayer and incense to all of them.

replies.title: Re: Deities from different pantheons
replies.content: Do you know of any books/articles that discuss this? The picture I'd gotten of the pre-Constantine Roman Empire (from professional historians and Orthodox hagiographies-the second an admittedly biased source) was that the attitude of Rome's "comfortable classes" towards Christianity was one of unrelenting hatred and persecution. Were there a lot of people who were able to combine worship of Christ with worship of the Roman gods? This seems rather fascinating...

replies.title: Re: Deities from different pantheons
replies.content: Hmm! I'll have to look for sources.

replies.title: Re: Deities from different pantheons
replies.content: In the 600s KIng Rædwald of East Anglia kept both an altar to Christ and an alter to the pagan Gods of his ancestors in his temple, as related by Bede in his Ecclesiastical History of the English People.  (Rædwald is most likely the king in the famous ship burial at Sutton Hoo.)

— Christophe

comments.title: Synchronicity
comments.content:
I read an interesting post on Dana O’Driscoll's blog (https://druidgarden.wordpress.com/2021/01/10/visioning-the-future-through-magical-arts/) recently where she talked about dystopian fiction possibly manifesting the futures they warned about. This got me wondering about optimism and visualization and mental 'hygiene'. As somebody who has always enjoyed immersing myself in dystopian fiction, collapse and doomerism I was wondering; are these things that are best avoided (with the aim of some form of mental purity) or can one partake in them with an aim towards balance with more hopeful pursuits?
I'm not sure if I should treat these 'negative' things like toxic waste (deadly) or more like mud (just dirty and can wash off). Any thoughts would be appreciated.


A shorter second question. Do you think that 'The Seal of Yueh Lao' had anything to do with the current resurgence in the popularity of sea shanties? I thought the timing was interesting.

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
1) Wallowing in doom from time to time won't harm you if you balance it with other things. If you spend all your time brooding over dystopia, you can help bring that about -- though it's worth noting that spending all your time brooding over utopias won't necessarily do anything to bring them about, and may simply result in you walking face first into a disastrous future. Treat your favorite doom fiction as over-the-top chocolate cake -- something to enjoy from time to time, but not as a steady diet!

2) I'm guessing you mean A Voyage to Hyperborea, which is where the pirates sing sea shanties. I have no idea if that's involved -- if it is, my tentacle fiction is being much more influential than I had any reason to suspect!

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
Thanks for the advice, I will try and treat wallowing in doom as chocolate cake and try to limit my intake accordingly.
Of course the shanties were a feature of 'A Voyage to Hyperborea'! it was actually one of my favourite books in the series making the title mix-up more embarrassing!

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content: Good heavens, don't worry about it. I sometimes have to look things up to remember which book something happened in!

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
I’ve also been wondering about that wallowing in doom of dystopian fictions. So many articles scoop up the dystopian buzzwords planted with precisely that intent by our overlords. The more they can get their myths repeated, the more... what? They can cause chaos in the basements of our psyches? They can make us fritter away our energies resisting their chimeras? They can lose all control as the myths take on unpredictable lives of their own?

It’s not so much repeating of the vapid, phatic phrases like “great reset” and “pandemic” I’m thinking of, but more the mini-narratives that get imbedded through the repetition of rebuttal and counter-argument. At this point, I don’t personally feel any need to read “you’ll own nothing and be happy™” or “a more infectious strain has been discovered™” ever again. They're mythologies, not news, and I don’t want to be part of bringing them into manifestation.

Yet their dystopian fantasies have weaseled their way into the news cycle, and, in fact, discussing them constitutes some of the more worthwhile analyses in the news today. How much does the thoughtful-end-of-the-media’s brooding over dystopias feed their likelihood? How much does exposure to their frenzied consternation hijack our will towards achieving the anti-goals they’re so distracted by?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content: That's part of it, but there's another dimension. Remember that the people we're talking about don't have any more self-awareness than their flacks and flunkeys. They repeat these things because that's what's babbling in their heads, too. That's what makes the barbarism of reflection so terrible a trap -- even those who supposedly run things are lost in a haze of abstractions and end up as sock puppets of crude emotional drives.

comments.title: Automatic banishing and etheric nudges
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG!

I hope your time off was refreshing and that you had the opportunity to do some of the things that your busy schedule usually doesn’t allow. Is there something particular you like to do while not on the keyboard (as much)?

I had a couple of interesting experiences which have made me rethink some things about the how I perceive the world and myself.

The first is that I was having a terrible day not long ago and while walking up to my bedroom a blue, flaming banishing pentagram was traced before me and all of the nasty feelings went away instantly. I thought that was pretty cool but was puzzled as to what happened. Was it me? It certainly was not intentionally triggered, I just stood there watching while it was being traced. It didn’t feel as some sort of reflex either, it felt as if an intelligence within (or from outside) was offering a helping hand. If it was me, then I am much more of what I think I am, not in the self-flattering way but in that there seems to be much more in a human being, the gods creation after all, than what we have been shown we are. The second one is sort of similar, but instead of my mind it was what I’d assume was my etheric body. It kind of nudged me into walking in a particular direction and I found some interesting things were I think it took me. Does this seem right? Are our own bodies and minds “intelligent’ in that kind of way or was it something else? If yes it has some interesting implications because seen that way, I am not as important in this game at all but rather it would seem that listening has a bigger role than what I would have to say about things and that is just my body, what would be left to say about the whole cosmos! Oddly enough, I would find that kind of relaxing and also humbling about this intricate design...

replies.title: Re: Automatic banishing and etheric nudges
replies.content:
Isn’t it a delight when other parts of the self or the higher self offer the conscious a helping hand? By the way, that has a common name — it’s called intuition. Your first example is a very dramatic form of intuition, but intuition nonetheless. There most certainly are more parts to the self than western culture dares teach us about. Why human beings are so intricately designed is a question that only the higher self could ever answer, but it does seem to enjoy the intricate game it has created.

Yes, our bodies and minds and the other parts of the self are quite “intelligent.” One way they are intelligent is in receiving and passing on images that come from others, so it’s not really an either-or question. The part that we’re designed to experience as “I” is nowhere near as important in the game as we’ve been encouraged to believe by the myth of individuality we were raised in. Thank the gods, since the “I” (the mind) is the least influential part of the self. (There are many definitions of mind, some of which distractingly encompass the entire self. I’m referring to the part of us that contemplates thoughts, whether consciously or subconsciously.)

Our self-flattering myth of individuality has left us walled off from eachother, the world, and ourselves. A different myth would give rise to a different reality: We are many, and each of us has unique gifts to contribute to the whole. You can see how that would create a profoundly different reality both internally and externally. You can also see how that myth has allowed John Michael to create the most vibrant online communities. Listening does indeed have a much bigger role when we become a plurality, contributing our unique gifts to a larger whole, a larger self. Oddly enough, it is kind of relaxing, and also humbling, and so very, very powerful. Welcome to the game.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) "Legitimate" is one of those awkward words that mean either too much or too little. I know people who've benefited greatly from Fourth Way work, if that's what you mean.

2) It's not a field I've looked into, and I've never taken the MBTI text. I prefer natal astrology as a source of self-knowledge!

3) The Golden Dawn in its heyday never conferred any grade higher than Adeptus Minor, and for good reason -- all the further stages of the Path must be achieved by the individual working with his or her Higher and Divine Genius. 4) I learned Tarot with reversals -- every source I knew about back in the 1970s used them as a matter of course. Tarot divination without reversals, to me, is like trying to write an essay with only half the letters in the alphabet.

replies.title: Tarot with reversals
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Tarot with reversals
replies.content:
It varies depending on what system of interpretations you use. The cards don't have rigidly fixed meanings, you know -- they are clusters of symbolism that can be interpreted in various ways.

In the system I originally learned, which came from Eden Gray's very good introductory books, the 4 of Pentacles (to pull out an example at random) when upright indicates wealth and influence without any spiritual or intellectual dimension at all; depending on its position in a reading, it can tell you that a gift or inheritance is on its way, or it can warn you that you're focusing too intently on material wealth and power and neglecting the rest of life. Reversed, it warns of obstacles, losses, and delays; it can caution you not to throw your money around freely.

replies.title: Re: Tarot with reversals
replies.content: Is it generally recommended to read the Tarot cards in such a mechanical and arbitrary way, with a handbook of interpretations at your side? One thing that doesn't get explained is how the authors of Tarot books derive their interpretations for the cards (I assume there's more to it than brute trial and error). My understanding is that there's an inner logic to the Tarot based on the Cabala and Tree of Life that allows you to understand them from first principles. Such an approach to the 4 of Pentacles (to continue with the examples) would see it as an expression of Chesed in Assiah (Jupiter) with all the connotations that brings to the mind of the student.

replies.title: Re: Tarot with reversals
replies.content: Sure, but getting to that inner logic requires a lot of meditation and study over a period of years. You can speed up the learning curve considerably by learning a set of established interpretations and adjusting those on the basis of your own intuition and insight.

replies.title: Marion Zimmer Bradley
replies.content:

Lewis portrayed Merlin in "That Hideous Strength," rang more true to me - I thought "this is the way a man like this in ancient Britain would really have thought and talked."

replies.title: Re: Marion Zimmer Bradley
replies.content:
I did a writing workshop with Bradley many years ago. She explained that the most important thing in genre writing was...market research. You find out what your audience wants and then give it to them. Her market research for The Mists of Avalon consisted of going to every Neopagan festival in northern California for a year, asking people what they thought the Arthurian legends were really about, and taking detailed notes. Thus MoA was a cheap rehash of the lowest common denominator of California Neopaganism in flimsy Arthurian drag.

I'm very fond of Arthurian legends from the Middle Ages, and one of the reasons for that is that they don't just feed our modern prejudices back to us. If you read Wolfram von Eschenbach's Parzival, for example, you're getting the thought of the Middle Ages as unfiltered as possible -- the adventures of knights written by a knight, somebody who knew what it was like to joust in armor because he did it.

Bradley's hackwork, by contrast, is a bunch of modern Californians prancing around in fancy dress and pretending to be ancient Celts. If I wanted that I'd hang out with -- well, let's not name names, shall we?

comments.title: Gnosticism
comments.content:
Welcome back JMG. I have spent most of January exploring Gnosticism, and it has led me to some interesting lines of thought on which I would appreciate your knowledgeable insight and direction. I have been drawn to Gnosticism as a means of practicing a mystical, esoteric Christianity. replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content:
1) "Gnosticism" is an extremely broad label for a wide range of esoteric spiritual traditions that emerged in the late classical world. About the only thing they all had in common was the conviction that gnosis (direct spiritual experience) rather than faith, works, or sacraments is the source of salvation. There's a lot of Gnosticism in the Western esoteric tradition, some via indirect channels and some very explicit -- there have been esoteric Gnostic churches active in the Western world for getting on for a couple of centuries now.

2) It's quite possible that something using Christian symbols and language, but with a Jungian-Gnostic core, could become a significant factor in the centuries ahead. I'm not sure whether it would count as Christian in more than a nominal sense, though.

comments.title: Vowels for five elements
comments.content: Hello JMG. If you had to assign the five Western vowels (AEIOU) to the five Hermetic elements, how would you do it and why? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Vowels for five elements
replies.content:
If I had to do that, I'd keep my attribution private, unless I wanted to introduce it to readers for some specific purpose. If someone asked me how to do it, I'd encourage them to meditate on the subject!

;-)

replies.title: Re: Vowels for five elements
replies.content:
If one takes sound spectrographs of various vowels, one finds that each one exhibits a few areas of relatively high energy, against the background of lower energy levels elsewhere in the spectrograph. These high-energy areas are called the formants of those vowels.

If you pronounce the vowels I E A O U (pronounced like English ee, eh, ah, oh, oo) in that order, their second formants have successively lower frequencies. Because of this, if you say (or, better, sing) them aloud and listen carefully, you will perceive a descending scale of notes. Reverse their order, and the scale of notes you perceive will be an ascending one.

The position of the tongue in the mouth also changes systematically as you run through the vowels iun this order, from the high front part of the mouth for I through the low central part for A to the high back part for U.

If you follow one of the two Medieval ways of assigning elements to the cardinal points of the compass, then Fire is in the South, Earth in the West, Water in the North, and Air in the East. As you move clockwise around this circle of cardinal points, the order of the elements will ascend, from the fire in the middle of the earth through earth and the waters that rest upon the earth to air that is above water (and earth), and then to the fire that is in the heavens. If, insteasd, you were to move counter-clockwise around the same circle, the order of the elements would descend.

And so you can use the vowels, sung or intoned, to reinforce your ascent or your descent through the elements.

In Ancient Greek there were seven vowels, not five, but their sounds show same patterns of ascent and descent. (You cannot, however, correlate them so easily with the four elements.) No doubt something of this sort was being used when, as Dionysius of Halicarnassus says, Egyptian priests of Serapis used a chant of just the vowels--he mentions the seven Greek ones--in their rituals.

Of course, there is no fifth element iun this scheme of things, so these remarks can do no more than serve as a signpost on your road to further meditations

[For sound spectrographs and formants, my resource was R. Jakobson, C. G. M. Fant and M. Halle, Preliminaries to Speech Analysis, Massachusetts Institute of Technonlgy, Acoustice Laboratory, 1955.]

replies.title: Re: Zodiac eastern, sun and moon, analog and digital and Philip K. Dick's Nirvana
replies.content:
I don't remember reading that Dick was diagnosed schizophrenic. Do you recall your source for that?

Be that as it may, there is probably a fine line between mental illness and creative genius fueled by intense visionary experience. I consider Philip K Dick's mystical experiences and his contact with what he called VALIS (Vast Active Living Intelligent System) to fall into the latter category.

I noticed in your response to another question on this week's Magic Monday that you used the phrase "black iron prison." As you are probably aware, Philip K Dick used that term in his novel VALIS to describe a concept of an all-pervasive system of social control.

If anyone is interested, selections from Dick's journal, in which he documented and explored his religious and visionary experiences, were published as The Exegesis of Philip K Dick in 2011.

His visionary experiences "provided him with the means to explore, with integrity, insight, and humility, the difficulties of making sense of any spiritual path in a relentlessly secular and cynical Western culture in which even apparent revelations can be instantly repackaged as popular entertainment." - Lawrence Sutin (author of Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K Dick; https://web.archive.org/web/20080410161534/http://www.philipkdick.com/aa_biography.html)
